title: Pelau
subtitle: Trinidadian caramelised one-pot rice with chicken and black-eyed beans
excerpt: Chicken pieces browned in caramelised sugar, then cooked in a single pot with coconut rice, black-eyed beans, and pumpkin. Trinidad's great one-pot dish — dark, sweet-savoury, and deeply fragrant.
sourceNotes: Pelau is a Trinidadian one-pot rice dish combining West African browning technique (caramelising sugar to colour the meat) with Caribbean rice-and-peas and coconut cooking traditions. Traditional pelau uses pigeon peas (fresh or dried); this recipe uses tinned black-eyed beans as a substitute, which are more widely available in the UK. Note: pigeon peas (gandules) should be added to the master ingredient table. Butternut squash substitutes for pumpkin, which may also need adding to the ingredient table.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freezes well in portions. Reheat with a splash of water in a covered pan, stirring gently.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The finished pelau keeps in the fridge for 3 days and reheats well.

content.text: Pelau is the dish that most Trinidadians would name as the definitive expression of the island's cooking. It is a single pot of rice that has absorbed the colour from caramelised sugar, the flavour of chicken and beans, the sweetness of coconut milk and pumpkin, and the aromatics of thyme, spring onion, and scotch bonnet. The result is deeply brown and fragrant, the rice grains separate and glossy, the chicken tender and sticky at the edges.

The caramelising step is the foundation: dark brown sugar melted in the oil until it smokes slightly, the chicken turned through it until every piece is coated in dark caramel. This is what gives pelau its characteristic colour. Take it far enough and you have a bitter edge that complements the sweetness of the coconut and pumpkin; stop too early and you have pale pelau, which lacks the depth.

items.prepNote: tinned, drained — pigeon peas if available
peeled and cut into 2 cm chunks — pumpkin if available

content.text: Heat the {{vegetable-oil}} of oil in a large Dutch oven over high heat. Add the {{dark-brown-sugar}} of dark brown sugar and stir until it melts into a dark, smoking caramel (about 2 minutes). Add all the {{chicken-thigh}} chicken thighs and {{chicken-drumsticks}} drumsticks at once and turn quickly to coat in the caramel. Work fast — the caramel burns if you hesitate. Brown the chicken on all sides, 4–5 minutes total.

Add the {{spring-onion}} spring onions, {{garlic}} of garlic, {{thyme-fresh}} of fresh thyme, and whole {{scotch-bonnet}} scotch bonnet. Stir for 1 minute. Add the {{black-eyed-beans}} of drained black-eyed beans, {{butternut-squash}} butternut squash chunks, {{long-grain-rice}} of rinsed rice, and {{coconut-milk}} of coconut milk. Add 300 ml of cold water. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Stir once to combine.

Bring to a boil, then reduce to the lowest simmer. Cover tightly and cook for 25 minutes without lifting the lid. After 25 minutes, check that the rice is cooked and the liquid has been absorbed. Remove from the heat and leave covered for 5 minutes. Remove the scotch bonnet and thyme before serving.

items.cause: Heat too high; too little liquid
items.fix: Pelau should always be cooked on the lowest possible heat after the initial boil. The bottom layer is traditionally allowed to form a slight crust — this is deliberate, called the bun-bun, and is considered the prize portion.

items.cause: Too much liquid; lid opened during cooking
items.fix: Do not lift the lid during the 25-minute steam. Once open, the steam escapes and the rice stalls. If rice is undercooked after 25 minutes, replace the lid and rest for 10 more minutes.
The caramel should be very dark brown, almost black, and smoking before the chicken goes in. Pale caramel = pale pelau with less flavour. Push the heat higher and wait for the smoke.

items.cause: Coconut milk too dominant; butternut squash very sweet
items.fix: Use 300 ml of coconut milk instead of the full tin and make up the difference with water. A squeeze of lime over the finished dish balances the sweetness.

content.text: Pelau is Sunday food and party food across Trinidad. It sits in the centre of the table at any significant gathering: a christening, a birthday, a cricket day, alongside macaroni pie and callaloo. The dish synthesises Trinidad's culinary history: the caramel-browning technique from West Africa, the rice and peas from the broader Caribbean tradition, the pigeon peas from the South American continent, the coconut and allspice from the island itself. It is the most Trinidadian dish there is.
